title: Microservices
description:
Microservices architecture structures systems as a set of small, independent, loosely coupled services, where each service implements a specific domain capability. These services are developed, deployed, and scaled autonomously, communicating through lightweight mechanisms, typically network-based. Its organization is based on the following conceptual blocks:
[ul]
[b]Independent Services[/b]: Autonomous units that encapsulate specific system functionalities, with their own logic control and often their own data.
[b]Service Communication[/b]: Carried out via APIs (e.g., HTTP/REST, messaging), allowing integration between distributed services.
[b]Management and Orchestration[/b]: Responsible for service discovery, load balancing, monitoring, and coordination (e.g., API Gateway, service registry).
[b]Distributed Infrastructure[/b]: Environment that supports service execution, including containers, orchestrators, and continuous deployment mechanisms.[/ul]
This architecture offers advantages such as: [b]good maintainability[/b], as smaller, isolated services facilitate understanding, testing, and independent evolution; and [b]high flexibility[/b], since each service can be deployed in different environments independently, favoring adaptation and scalability.
A disadvantage is [b]low performance[/b] in certain scenarios, due to network communication overhead, latency between services, and the need for distributed synchronization. Moreover, the distributed nature can increase operational complexity and require additional management mechanisms.
concept: System composed of small independent services that communicate over the network.
advantage: Scalability and Evolution. Each service can be updated or replaced without stopping the others.
disadvantage: Network Complexity. Latency from distributed communication can harm performance.

bullet_point.bullet2: Each service has its own responsibility
